data.title: JD.com proposes CNY-denominated senior notes sale, details to follow
data.description:
• JD.com (JD) plans to raise funds through a proposed offshore issuance of CNY-denominated senior unsecured notes, with final deal terms to be determined at pricing.
  • Net proceeds will be used for general corporate purposes, including repayment of certain existing indebtedness and payment of interest.
  • The Chinese e-commerce major will offer the notes to non-U.S. investors in offshore transactions with details such as principal amount, coupon, and maturity yet to be finalized and subject to market conditions.

data.title: Scorpio Tankers to sell two MR tankers for $70 million, closing expected in Q2
data.description:
• Scorpio Tankers (STNG) has entered into agreements to sell two 2015-built scrubber-fitted MR product tankers, STI Brooklyn and STI Black Hawk, for $35.0 million per vessel.
  • The sale of these vessels is expected to close within the second quarter of 2026.

data.title: John B. Sanfilippo & Son declares $1.50 special dividend
data.description:
• John B. Sanfilippo & Son (JBSS) declares $1.50/share special dividend.
  • Payable May 21; for shareholders of record April 27; ex-div April 27.
  • See JBSS Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: American Exchange Group to acquire Allbirds assets in $39M transaction
Versigent to replace Titan International in S&P SmallCap 600
data.description:
• Versigent plc will replace Titan International (TWI) in the S&P SmallCap 600 (SP600) effective prior to the opening of trading on April 2, 2026.
  • S&P 500 constituent Aptiv (APTV) is spinning off Versigent in a transaction expected to be completed April 1. Post spin-off, Aptiv will remain in the S&P 500.
  • Titan International's market capitalization is no longer representative of the small cap market space.

data.title: Delaware judge Kathaleen McCormick reassigns Elon Musk cases after bias claims
data.description:
• Judge Kathaleen McCormick said Monday she is reassigning cases involving Elon Musk after he alleged bias stemming from what appeared to be her support of a social media post critical of him.
  • Musk formally accused McCormick of bias last week, and his attorneys demanded that the Delaware Court of Chancery judge recuse herself from two Tesla (TSLA) lawsuits. The LinkedIn post that McCormick allegedly responded to with an emoji touted a court verdict that could cost Musk upwards of $2 billion for defrauding Twitter investors.
  • "As should be obvious, disproportionate media ​attention surrounding a judge’s handling of an action is detrimental to the administration of justice," McCormick wrote in her letter ​to the legal teams in the three cases, adding she was not biased against the defendants, according to a Reuters report. She also ⁠said the motion for her recusal was based on the false premise that she was supportive of the LinkedIn post.
  • In a letter to the legal teams last week, McCormick said she had not read the post and reported the incident as suspicious activity to LinkedIn. The defendants said ​if McCormick eventually sided with Tesla's (TSLA) shareholders, her ruling would be tainted by her "support" for the people standing up to Musk.

data.title: Pentagon calls report on Hegseth BlackRock defense fund bets before Iran war as ‘false and fabricated’
data.description:
A broker for U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth sought to make a large investment in major defense firms in the lead-up to the Iran war, according to the Financial Times. The Pentagon has dismissed the report.

The FT reported Tuesday that Hegseth’s broker at banking giant Morgan Stanley (MS) contacted BlackRock (BLK) in February about making a multimillion-dollar investment in its iShares Defense Industrials Active ETF (IDEF).

Pentagon chief spokesperson Sean Parnell dismissed the FT report in a post on X, calling it “entirely false and fabricated,” and demanding the FT retract the article.

Parnell said that neither Hegseth nor any of his representatives approached BlackRock (BLK) about any such investment. “This is yet another baseless, dishonest smear designed to mislead the public,” he added. 

The investment discussed by Hegseth’s broker did not ultimately go ahead.

The ETF, which has about $3.1 billion in assets, counts companies such as RTX Corp. (RTX), — formerly known as Raytheon — Lockheed Martin (LMT) and Northrop Grumman (NOC) among its largest holdings, Blackrock data showed.

data.title: Virgin Galactic targets commercial service launch in Q4 2026 as flight cadence ramps to 10-plus per month by mid-2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Virgin Galactic Holdings, Inc. (SPCE) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Michael Colglazier highlighted the completion of structural assembly for all three major components of the company’s new spaceship, stating the “weight on wheels milestone for this first ship is expected in the next few weeks,” with ground test phase to begin in April and flight tests on track for Q3. He confirmed the first commercial spaceflight is expected in Q4 2026, and that “we have opened the sales window for Virgin Galactic Spaceflight Expeditions, and we are now adding people to our Spacefarer community at new price points.”
  • Colglazier announced the hiring of Chief Growth Officer Megan Prichard, whose remit includes “scaling our business at Spaceport America, establishing additional revenue streams for our existing and emerging technology, building brand partnerships and accelerating the development of new spaceports.”
  • The CEO emphasized the company’s enhanced production capabilities, stating, “we now have the infrastructure and capability to build and assemble spaceships efficiently, reliably and at scale. This provides an enormous competitive advantage as we grow our business.”
  • CFO Douglas Ahrens stated, “in December, we successfully executed an exchange with several of the holders of our 2027 convertible bonds, addressing $355 million of the $425 million... These transactions were done very intentionally and with capital preservation in mind.”

Outlook

  • Colglazier indicated the company expects to commence commercial spaceflight operations at “approximately 4 spaceflights per month,” ramping to an average of 8 spaceflights per month, and targeting “10-plus flights per month sometime in the second quarter of 2027.”
  • Ahrens shared projections for Q1 2026 revenue of “approximately $200,000 for astronaut access fees” and forecasted free cash flow for Q1 2026 “in the range of negative $90 million to $95 million.” He stated the company expects “modestly positive quarterly cash flow within 2027, with positive cash flow forecasted to scale in 2028 and beyond.”

Financial Results

  • Ahrens reported Q4 2025 revenue of $300,000, operating expenses of $61 million (a 26% reduction from the prior year), a net loss of $63 million (18% improvement), and adjusted EBITDA of negative $49 million (23% improvement). Free cash flow was negative $95 million, a 19% improvement.
  • For the full year 2025, revenue was $2 million, total operating expenses $287 million, net loss $279 million, and adjusted EBITDA negative $226 million. Free cash flow was negative $438 million.
  • Year-end cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities stood at $338 million. Capital expenditures for 2025 were $198 million, with property, plant, and equipment of $389 million at year-end.

Q&A

  • Oliver Chen, TD Cowen: Asked about the Chief Growth Officer’s roadmap, CapEx trajectory, new spaceport economics, and commercial spaceflight revenue guidance. Colglazier responded Megan Prichard’s “remit is much broader,” including sales growth and new spaceport partnerships, with joint agreements likely. Ahrens said CapEx is “front-end loaded in the first half of the year and then it tapers off,” and Q4 2026 revenue will depend on start timing and ticket mix, with an initial cadence of “about one flight a week.”
  • Greg Konrad, Jefferies: Asked about the next mothership timeline and ticket sales strategy. Colglazier said “2030 is the right timing for a next launch vehicle,” and explained that sales tranches are limited to support pricing strategy, not driven by backlog.
  • Myles Walton, Wolfe Research: Inquired about the flight test sequence and going concern disclosure. Michael Moses clarified, “1 rocket-powered flight that’s not full duration... then 2 spaceflights before we enter commercial service,” with the first test flight carrying research payloads. Colglazier explained partnership discussions for funding future vehicle development. Ahrens clarified the company’s cash flow terminology.
  • Michael Leshock, KeyBanc: Asked about free cash flow improvement and defense technology use cases. Ahrens said improvements will be “gradual until the fourth quarter,” with a “big change” when customer payments come in. Colglazier noted the company is qualified as a supplier for the Golden Dome initiative but had “nothing specific to share.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on execution risks, cash burn, flight cadence, pricing, and competitive positioning, with a neutral to slightly positive tone as they pressed for clarity on growth and capital plans.
  • Management’s tone in prepared remarks was confident, highlighting progress and future milestones. During Q&A, responses remained detailed and constructive, with Colglazier calling Megan Prichard “an amazing executive” and Ahrens providing transparent guidance on spending. They were direct about risks, using phrases like “we expect,” “we plan,” and “we have built.”
  • Compared to last quarter, management’s tone showed increased confidence, especially regarding operational ramp and financial runway. Analyst tone remained consistent, with continued focus on execution timelines and financial discipline.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter provided more detailed operational timelines, including the start of ground tests in April, flight tests in Q3, and first commercial flight in Q4 2026, compared to last quarter’s broader ranges.
  • Management announced the hiring of a Chief Growth Officer and opened ticket sales at a new $750,000 price point, compared to previous updates on digital relaunch and sales strategy.
  • Free cash flow and cost control improvements were highlighted, with reductions in operating expenses and net loss, while the previous quarter emphasized ramping CapEx and property investment.
  • Analysts continued to focus on flight cadence ramp and cash flow milestones, with added questions about new spaceports and defense opportunities.
  • Strategic focus shifted toward scaling operations, international expansion, and cash-positive operations by 2027, building on last quarter’s readiness themes.

Risks and Concerns

  • Colglazier acknowledged remaining risks around testing, production, and launch timing, as well as the need to “operate with safety in mind and prudence.”
  • Management highlighted the ongoing assessment of “going concern” in the 10-K, noting that the accounting methodology does not yet account for expected future cash inflows from ticket sales and spaceflights.
  • Analysts queried sensitivities in flight cadence, CapEx trajectory, and the competitive landscape, while management pointed to mitigating strategies such as flexible production, joint spaceport agreements, and limited high-priced ticket tranches.

Final Takeaway

Virgin Galactic management emphasized that 2026 is a pivotal year as the company transitions from pre-revenue to commercial operations, with the first new spaceship entering ground and flight testing ahead of a targeted Q4 launch. The ramp in flight cadence, new higher-priced ticket sales, and the addition of a Chief Growth Officer are expected to drive growth, with projections for positive quarterly cash flow in 2027 and scalable profitability beyond. Management stressed the company’s strengthened balance sheet, operational readiness, and focus on expanding its astronaut community and international footprint as catalysts for long-term value creation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Phreesia outlines $510M–$520M revenue target for 2027 amid network solutions headwinds and AccessOne integration
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Phreesia, Inc. (PHR) Q4 2026

Management View

  • Chaim Indig, Co-Founder, CEO & Director, highlighted that fiscal year 2026 was pivotal, marked by deliberate choices and disciplined execution. He stated, "We surpassed $100 million in adjusted EBITDA. We crossed $50 million in free cash flow. And for the first time in our history as a publicly traded company, we delivered positive GAAP net income for a full fiscal year." Indig also cited the acquisition of AccessOne and the launch of Provider Connect as milestones, explaining that AccessOne is performing in line with expectations and has reinforced the investment thesis. Regarding Provider Connect, Indig noted, "We believe our ability to align both sides of the care conversation is something no one else in the market can do as comprehensively as Phreesia."
  • Indig emphasized the company’s ability to grow in a tough market and referenced external challenges, such as FDA guidelines, insurance coverage, and provider reimbursement, as well as shorter visibility into spending commitments from pharmaceutical manufacturers within the Network Solutions business. He added, "While we do not believe this reflects a structural shift in demand for what Phreesia offers, it is creating more variability in our financial forecast."
  • Balaji Gandhi, Chief Financial Officer, reported, "For the fourth quarter of fiscal year 2026, revenue was $127.1 million, up 16% year-over-year with growth led by Payment Solutions following the acquisition of AccessOne." He mentioned adjusted EBITDA of $29.4 million and noted that free cash flow reached $28.5 million, the company’s strongest quarterly free cash flow to date. Gandhi detailed the completion of a refinancing, replacing the bridge loan with a new $275 million senior secured revolving credit facility.

Outlook

  • Gandhi stated, "We are lowering our revenue outlook for fiscal year 2027. We now expect revenue to be in the range of $510 million to $520 million compared to our prior range of $545 million to $559 million." The update is attributed to reduced spending commitments by certain Network Solutions clients, especially in the second half of the year.
  • The company is maintaining its adjusted EBITDA outlook for fiscal 2027 at $125 million to $135 million, despite the lower revenue guidance. Gandhi explained, "It is worth noting that we are holding our adjusted EBITDA outlook even as we reduce our revenue range, a reflection of the operating leverage we have built and our ability to respond quickly with further efficiency gains."
  • Gandhi reported maintaining AHSC growth expectations in the mid-single-digit percentage range for fiscal 2027, while updating total revenue per AHSC to a low single-digit percentage range due to Network Solutions headwinds.

Financial Results

  • Gandhi stated, "Fourth quarter average healthcare services clients, or AHSCs, reached 4,658, an increase of 138% from the prior quarter. 80 of these AHSCs were contributed to the AccessOne acquisition."
  • Total revenue per AHSC was $27,279, up 8% year-over-year. Net cash provided by operating activities was $33.7 million, and cash and cash equivalents as of January 31, 2026, were $73.8 million compared to $84.2 million at January 31, 2025.
  • Gandhi highlighted that the year-over-year improvements in operating cash flow and free cash flow were driven primarily by changes in working capital and contributions from AccessOne.

Q&A

  • Sean Dodge, BMO Capital Markets: Asked for clarification on Network Solutions guidance changes and whether reduced spending visibility was broad-based. Gandhi replied, "It is not broad-based. It is in specific brand and therapeutic areas. Vaccines...clearly, vaccine spending and targeted marketing around that has been pulled back."
  • Ryan Daniels, William Blair: Asked about Provider Connect’s contribution. Gandhi responded, "Very little. Very early days...the launch went well, and we do see some upside there."
  • Jailendra Singh, Truist Securities: Inquired about the nature of cost actions supporting unchanged EBITDA guidance. Gandhi attributed margin expansion to structural efficiency improvements, particularly through AI, and highlighted ongoing efforts to drive margin through reduced manual processes and outsourcing.
  • Jessica Tassan, Piper Sandler: Asked about payment processing growth outside AccessOne. Gandhi said, "We expect it to grow year-over-year exclusive of AccessOne...it will grow in the single digits."
  • Ryan MacDonald, Needham & Company: Asked about scaling AccessOne and priorities for growth. Gandhi emphasized both tighter integration within Phreesia and expansion into new markets, stating, "That is also super important...we've been very active on expanding the capital base."
  • Steven Valiquette, Mizuho Securities: Asked about GLP-1 drugs’ impact on Network Solutions. Gandhi said, "On the margin, they're in the bad guy category as you would characterize it."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns over visibility in Network Solutions revenue, asking detailed, sometimes skeptical questions about guidance reductions and the sustainability of cost actions. Their tone was neutral to slightly negative, seeking clarity on segment-level impacts, especially regarding pharmaceutical spending.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone in prepared remarks, but in Q&A, Gandhi’s responses reflected a more cautious and transparent approach, admitting increased variability and fluidity in certain revenue streams. Gandhi’s use of phrases like "very fluid" and "we want to be transparent" indicated openness but also reflected increased uncertainty compared to the previous quarter.
  • Compared to Q3, management’s sentiment shifted from strong confidence in consistent growth to a more measured tone, acknowledging specific headwinds and being proactive in communicating changes to outlook.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue guidance for fiscal 2027 was reduced from $545–$559 million in Q3 to $510–$520 million in Q4, due to lower visibility and spending commitments in Network Solutions, particularly from pharmaceutical clients in certain therapeutic areas.
  • Adjusted EBITDA guidance remained unchanged at $125–$135 million, highlighting improved operational efficiency and cost discipline.
  • Management’s tone evolved from emphasizing broad-based growth and new product launches in Q3 to focusing on resilience and adaptability amid external headwinds in Q4.
  • Analysts in the current quarter focused more acutely on the drivers behind guidance reductions and sustainability of margin expansion, compared to broader growth and acquisition integration discussions in the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted shorter visibility into spending commitments from pharmaceutical manufacturers, particularly in Network Solutions, as a significant risk, leading to increased revenue forecast variability.
  • Regulatory changes and public health dynamics, especially regarding vaccines and specific therapeutic categories like GLP-1 drugs, were cited as drivers of reduced commitments.
  • Gandhi explained that these headwinds are "not a broad-based" challenge but are concentrated in specific areas and are being factored transparently into guidance.

Final Takeaway

Phreesia’s fourth quarter call revealed both significant progress and new challenges, as the company delivered positive GAAP net income for the first time and achieved record free cash flow, while also lowering revenue expectations for fiscal 2027 amid softness in Network Solutions. Management remains confident in its operating leverage and ability to drive margin expansion, supported by AI-enabled efficiencies and the integration of AccessOne, while proactively communicating the impact of reduced pharmaceutical spending commitments and maintaining a disciplined approach to capital allocation and growth initiatives.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Tuesday’s Economic Calendar
data.description:
9:00 AM Case-Shiller Home Price Index

The S&P CoreLogic Case-Shiller Home Price Index tracks monthly changes in the value of residential real estate in 20 metropolitan regions across the nation. Composite indexes and regional indexes measure changes in existing home prices and are based on single-family home resales.

9:00 AM FHFA House Price Index

The Federal Housing Finance Agency (FHFA) House Price Index (HPI) covers single-family housing, using data provided by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac. 

9:45 AM Chicago PMI

The Institute for Supply Management - Chicago compiles a survey and a composite diffusion index of business conditions in the Chicago area.

After a remarkable pop into positive territory at 57.7 in February, the consensus sees March holding strong at 54.0. After years of contraction, this is difficult to fathom.

10:00 AM Consumer Confidence

The Conference Board's confidence report surveys consumers on their assessments of the labor market, business activity, and their own financial conditions.

The consensus sees confidence down at 88.5 in March versus 91.1 in February and 89.0 in January. Worries about inflation and jobs continue to weigh, and the Iran war is darkening the picture for consumers.

10:00 AM JOLTS 

The Labor Department's JOLTS report tracks monthly change in job openings and offers rates on hiring and quits. 

12:00 PM Austan Goolsbee Speaks

Chicago Federal Reserve Bank President Austan Goolsbee gives opening remarks before the "Human Capital in a Time of Low Real Rates" event hosted by the Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago.

1:10 PM Jeff Schmid Speaks

Kansas City (KS) Federal Reserve Bank President Jeff Schmid speaks on monetary policy and the economic outlook at the Rotary Club of Oklahoma City.

3:00 PM Michael Barr Speaks

Federal Reserve Board Governor Michael Barr speaks on "Stablecoins" at The Federalist Society's The GENIUS Act in Practice: Key Questions for Stablecoin Regulation.

5:10 PM Michelle Bowman Speaks

Federal Reserve Vice Chair for Supervision Michelle Bowman speaks on "Small Business" at CBA Live 2026.
